date.note: A collection, not a single-date work. Its constituent books were composed and edited over many centuries (roughly the early first millennium BCE into the Second Temple period), and its threefold shape and contents took form gradually; the Torah was authoritative earliest, with the Prophets and then the Writings settling later. No single year applies; the tripartite structure is attested by the late Second Temple period.

summary: The Tanakh is the Hebrew Bible, the tripartite Jewish scripture whose name is an acronym of its three parts: the Torah (Law or Instruction), the Nevi'im (Prophets), and the Ketuvim (Writings). It is the foundational scripture of Judaism, read and interpreted on its own terms and in its own arrangement, distinct from the Christian Old Testament and from the Islamic notion of the Tawrat.
description: The Tanakh is the collection of writings that constitutes the Hebrew Bible in Judaism. Its name, TaNaKh, is an acronym formed from the initial letters of its three divisions: the Torah, the Nevi'im, and the Ketuvim. The Torah (the Law, Instruction, or Teaching) comprises the five books of Moses, Genesis, Exodus, Leviticus, Numbers, and Deuteronomy, and holds the highest authority in the tradition. The Nevi'im (the Prophets) contains the Former Prophets (Joshua, Judges, Samuel, and Kings, largely narrative histories) and the Latter Prophets (Isaiah, Jeremiah, Ezekiel, and the Twelve minor prophets). The Ketuvim (the Writings) is a varied collection that includes the Psalms, Proverbs, and Job, the Five Scrolls (Song of Songs, Ruth, Lamentations, Ecclesiastes, and Esther), and Daniel, Ezra-Nehemiah, and Chronicles. As Marc Zvi Brettler stresses, the Tanakh is a library assembled over a long span rather than a single book, its component texts composed and edited across many centuries and shaped by the culture that produced them; the tradition receives it as sacred scripture (Miqra, that which is read) in a fixed order and canon. The threefold division is already reflected in Jewish sources of the late Second Temple period, and Lawrence Schiffman traces how the biblical writings moved from text to authoritative tradition in this era, read in synagogue, translated into Greek for the diaspora as the Septuagint, and interpreted through the developing traditions of the period. The Tanakh is the Jewish scripture on its own terms. Its content overlaps with the Christian Old Testament, but Judaism orders the books differently (closing with Chronicles rather than a prophetic book) and its canon differs from the various Christian canons, and it is not to be conflated with the Christian Old Testament nor with the Tawrat of Islamic teaching, which is the Islamic tradition's own conception of the scripture given to Moses; the Christian and Islamic readings of these writings are attributed to those traditions and are not fused into the Jewish description here.
editorial_notes: This entry describes the Tanakh on its own terms per neutrality principles #1, #4, and #6, and per ADR-0008 keeps the Christian and Islamic readings attributed to those traditions rather than fusing them into the Jewish description. The acronym is spelled out (Torah, Nevi'im, Ketuvim); the three-part structure and the distinctive Jewish ordering (ending with Chronicles) distinguish the Tanakh from the Christian Old Testament, whose ordering and canon differ, and from the Islamic Tawrat, which is a distinct theological category and not simply a synonym for the Torah. No single composition date is given because the Tanakh is a multi-authored collection edited over centuries; this is stated in the date note rather than forced into a year. Sources triangulate three lenses per neutrality principles #8-9: a contemporary Jewish scholar of the Hebrew Bible (Brettler), an academic historian of the Second Temple and rabbinic periods for the formation of scripture (Schiffman), and the primary text itself (the Tanakh / Masoretic Text). The relation targets torah and tawrat are forward references to entities not yet created; second-temple-judaism is authored alongside this entry. VERIFIED (2026-07-19): the tripartite structure and the distinctive Jewish ordering (Torah, Nevi'im, Ketuvim, ending with Chronicles) were read directly and confirmed on Sefaria, and the late-Second-Temple attestation against the directly-read Prologue to Ben Sira ('the Law and the Prophets and the other books'); the Masoretic-text source is raised to high and a Ben Sira source added at high, and the entry is promoted to verified. Brettler and Schiffman remain medium (not read line by line).

relations.note: The writings of the Tanakh took on authoritative scriptural status during the Second Temple period, and its threefold structure is attested by the late Second Temple period.
The Torah, the five books of Moses, is the first and most authoritative division of the Tanakh (the T of TaNaKh). Forward reference to a not-yet-created entity.

Shared theme: a scripture associated with Moses and the law. Difference: the Tanakh is the Jewish tripartite Hebrew Bible received in a fixed Jewish order and canon, whereas the Tawrat is the Islamic tradition's own conception of the scripture given to Moses; the terms are not synonyms, and neither reading is treated as the measure of the other (neutrality #10). Forward reference to a not-yet-created entity in another tradition.

sources.citation: The Prologue to the Wisdom of Ben Sira (Sirach), late 2nd century BCE, which refers three times to 'the Law and the Prophets and the other books' (and 'the others that followed them'). The earliest explicit attestation of the tripartite shape of the Hebrew scriptures, supporting the entry's late-Second-Temple dating claim.
sources.license: Ancient text in the public domain (English translations vary). The Prologue read directly (2026-07-19) and confirmed.

sources.citation: Brettler, Marc Zvi. How to Read the Jewish Bible. Oxford: Oxford University Press, 2007 (orig. published as How to Read the Bible, Jewish Publication Society, 2005). Contemporary Jewish scholar's introduction to reading the Hebrew Bible in its historical and literary context; cited for the Tanakh as a collection composed and edited over centuries and read on Jewish terms. Verified to exist and be on-topic, not read line-by-line.

Schiffman, Lawrence H. From Text to Tradition: A History of Second Temple and Rabbinic Judaism. Hoboken, NJ: Ktav Publishing House, 1991. Academic history tracing the biblical heritage and the movement from scriptural text to authoritative tradition across the Second Temple and rabbinic periods; cited for the formation and authoritative reception of scripture. Verified to exist and be on-topic, not read line-by-line.

The Tanakh (Hebrew Bible), Masoretic Text: Torah, Nevi'im, and Ketuvim. Primary scripture; cited as the primary source for its own tripartite structure, contents, and ordering. English reference translation: JPS Hebrew-English Tanakh, 2nd ed., Philadelphia: Jewish Publication Society, 1999.
sources.license: Hebrew text in the public domain; the JPS translation is proprietary. Structure, contents, and the distinctive Jewish ordering (ending with Chronicles) read directly and confirmed on Sefaria (2026-07-19); individual books not read in full.
